story:
Our fourth beach trip was the one that broke me in half. It was early August, hot and bright, and Tyler and I were heading out for his birthday tradition—three days by the water, just us and the sunrise he loved. While I packed half my closet, he laughed. “Kennedy, we’re only going for three days.” I shot back, “I need more than a toothbrush and one pair of underwear.” From the kitchen, his mom called, “Now, Tyler, I taught you better than that!” We all cracked up.

We kissed our German shepherds, Kirk and Spock, goodbye—his mom would spoil them while we were gone—and hit the road. At the first gas stop he leaned out the window, grinning, “Hey, what’s your name? Can I get your number?” I laughed. “Check your phone—it might already be in there.” We blasted 80s music the whole way, him drumming the wheel, me singing wildly off-key, both of us pretending not to love it.

We went straight to my parents’ beach house and, starving, found a little seafood place. I’ve never been big on it, but Tyler grew up eating everything and loved catfish most of all. That night, even I cleaned my plate. On the walk back, I told him we had to turn in so we could catch the sunrise. He smiled and said, “You are the sunrise I love.” I melted. Back at the house we showered, tugged on the matching pajamas he “hated” but wore for me anyway, and clinked a couple of birthday shots. “Cheers to twenty-eight,” he said. We curled up, warm and close, and drifted off before midnight.

At 5 a.m. his alarm buzzed. I kissed his cheek. “Happy birthday.” I cooked bacon, eggs, fried potatoes, and French toast—his favorite—then we carried it to the sand. The horizon bloomed gold and pink while we ate and talked about everything and nothing. For a moment it felt like time stopped. I closed my eyes and thought, this couldn’t possibly get any better.

We napped after and woke at four. He had 6:30 reservations. I took forever getting ready; when I stepped out, he just stared. “Well, damn. I didn’t know I had the most beautiful girl on the planet.” I blushed. The restaurant was quiet luxury—marble floors, velvet seats, candlelight, the low hum of an open kitchen. During the last course he started sweating, even though it was cool inside. He smiled, excused himself, and, I learned later, steadied his breathing and checked the velvet box one more time.

When he came back, everything else disappeared. He stopped beside my chair, dropped to one knee, and asked the question he’d rehearsed a thousand times. “Oh my God—are you serious right now?!” I laughed through tears. “Yes. Yes, of course I’ll marry you.” The ring caught the candlelight and my hands shook. I kissed him and whispered, “You’re never getting rid of me now.”

We floated out into the night, my new ring flashing under streetlights. He opened my door, kissed my hand, and said softly, “Forever starts now.”

We pulled onto the road. A blast of headlights—too fast, too close. Screeching tires. Shattering glass. The world spun and then went still. Rain began to fall. I reached for him in the broken quiet, his hand limp in mine. “Did anyone see that?” I cried to the empty, wet street. My ring glimmered in the dark—once a beginning, now the last light on everything we’d planned to be.
